cards.definition: a family consisting of two parents and their dependent children living together
a family that includes relatives beyond the nuclear family — e.g. grandparents, aunts, uncles
a family with one parent and dependent children — the most common alternative to the nuclear family in the UK
a family formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together
a family with two parents of the same gender
the legal ending of a marriage — divorce rates have risen since the Divorce Reform Act 1969
living together as a couple without being married — increasingly common in the UK
a conservative political perspective that sees the traditional nuclear family as the ideal family structure
a sociological perspective that focuses on gender inequality — argues the family often oppresses women
unpaid work done in the home such as cooking, cleaning, and childcare — disproportionately done by women

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a nuclear family?
options: Two parents and their dependent children living together
A family that includes grandparents, aunts, and uncles
A family with one parent and dependent children
A family formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together
questions.correct_answer: Two parents and their dependent children living together
questions.explanation: A nuclear family is the basic family unit consisting of just parents and their children living in one household.

questions.question: What is an extended family?
options: A family with same-sex parents
A family that includes relatives beyond the nuclear family such as grandparents and aunts
A family formed when divorced parents remarry
Two people living together without being married
questions.correct_answer: A family that includes relatives beyond the nuclear family such as grandparents and aunts
questions.explanation: An extended family goes beyond just parents and children to include other blood relatives living together or in close contact.

questions.question: Which family type consists of one parent and dependent children?
options: Reconstituted family
Extended family
Single-parent family
Same-sex family
questions.explanation: A single-parent family has only one parent raising the dependent children, and this is the most common alternative to the nuclear family in the UK.

questions.question: What is a reconstituted family also known as?
options: A blended family
A traditional family
An extended family
A cohabiting family
questions.explanation: A reconstituted or blended family is formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together as a family unit.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a same-sex family?
options: A mother and father with two children
Two mothers and their adopted children
A single mother with one child
A grandmother, parents, and children living together
questions.correct_answer: Two mothers and their adopted children
questions.explanation: A same-sex family has two parents of the same gender, such as two mothers or two fathers, raising children together.

questions.question: When did divorce rates start to rise significantly in the UK?
options: After the Divorce Reform Act 1969
After World War II
In the 1950s
In the 1980s
questions.explanation: The Divorce Reform Act 1969 made it easier to get divorced, which led to a significant increase in divorce rates in the UK.

questions.question: What does cohabitation mean?
options: Being married with children
Living together as a couple without being married
Having an extended family living together
Being in a same-sex relationship
questions.correct_answer: Living together as a couple without being married
questions.explanation: Cohabitation is when couples live together in a long-term relationship without getting married, which has become increasingly common in modern UK society.

questions.question: What do the New Right believe about family structure?
options: All family types are equally valid
Extended families are superior to nuclear families
The traditional nuclear family is the ideal family structure
Single-parent families are the best for children
questions.correct_answer: The traditional nuclear family is the ideal family structure
questions.explanation: The New Right is a conservative political perspective that values the traditional nuclear family as the best and most important family type.

questions.question: What does feminism argue about families?
options: Families are always equal and fair
The family often oppresses women and focuses on gender inequality
Nuclear families are perfect
Women should not work outside the home
questions.correct_answer: The family often oppresses women and focuses on gender inequality
questions.explanation: Feminism is a sociological perspective that highlights how families can perpetuate gender inequality and argue that women are often disadvantaged within family structures.

questions.question: What is domestic labour?
options: Working in a factory
Unpaid work done in the home such as cooking, cleaning, and childcare
Working for the government
Paid employment outside the home
questions.correct_answer: Unpaid work done in the home such as cooking, cleaning, and childcare
questions.explanation: Domestic labour refers to the unpaid household work like cooking, cleaning, and looking after children, which sociologists note is disproportionately done by women.

questions.question: A nuclear family always includes grandparents living in the same house.
questions.explanation: A nuclear family consists only of two parents and their dependent children. If grandparents live with them, it would be an extended family.

questions.question: Single-parent families are the most common alternative to nuclear families in the UK.
questions.explanation: Single-parent families, where one parent raises dependent children, are indeed the most common alternative family type to the traditional nuclear family in modern UK society.

questions.question: Reconstituted families are created when two unmarried people start dating.
questions.explanation: Reconstituted families are specifically formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together, not just any dating couple.

questions.question: Cohabitation has become increasingly common in the UK.
questions.explanation: Cohabitation, where couples live together without being married, is becoming more and more common in modern UK society.

questions.question: The New Right believes that all family types are equally good.
questions.explanation: The New Right is a conservative perspective that specifically values the traditional nuclear family as superior to other family types.

questions.question: Feminism focuses on how gender inequality affects family life.
questions.explanation: Feminism is a sociological perspective that examines gender inequality and argues that families often perpetuate unequal power relations between men and women.

questions.question: Domestic labour is always paid work.
questions.explanation: Domestic labour is specifically unpaid work done in the home, such as cooking, cleaning, and childcare.

questions.question: Same-sex families can only consist of two parents without children.
questions.explanation: Same-sex families can include two parents of the same gender with dependent children, either adopted or from previous relationships.

questions.question: The Divorce Reform Act 1969 made it harder to get divorced.
questions.explanation: The Divorce Reform Act 1969 actually made it easier to obtain a divorce, which led to increased divorce rates in the UK.

questions.question: Extended families include aunts and uncles as part of the household.
questions.explanation: Extended families include relatives beyond the nuclear family such as grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins, often living together or in close contact.

questions.question: A ________ family consists of two parents and their dependent children living together.
questions.explanation: A nuclear family is the most basic family unit with just parents and children.

questions.question: An ________ family includes grandparents, aunts, and uncles as part of the family unit.
questions.explanation: Extended families go beyond the nuclear family to include other blood relatives.

questions.question: A ________ family has one parent and dependent children.
questions.explanation: Single-parent families consist of one adult raising children, whether due to divorce, death, or other circumstances.

questions.question: A ________ family is formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together.
questions.explanation: Reconstituted families, also called blended families, are created when previously separated people with children form a new household.

questions.question: Living together as a couple without being married is called ________.
questions.explanation: Cohabitation is the term for unmarried couples living together in a long-term relationship.

questions.question: The ________ is a conservative perspective that sees the traditional nuclear family as ideal.
questions.explanation: The New Right is a political perspective that values traditional family structures and opposes alternative family types.

questions.question: ________ is a sociological perspective that focuses on gender inequality in families.
questions.explanation: Feminism examines how gender inequalities are created and maintained within family structures.

questions.question: ________ refers to unpaid work such as cooking, cleaning, and childcare done in the home.
questions.explanation: Domestic labour is the housework and care work performed in the home, typically done more by women.

questions.question: A ________ family consists of two parents of the same gender.
questions.explanation: Same-sex families have two parents of the same gender, either two mothers or two fathers.

questions.question: Since the ________ 1969, divorce rates in the UK have increased significantly.
questions.explanation: The Divorce Reform Act 1969 made divorce easier to obtain, resulting in higher divorce rates.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Living together as a couple without being married
Unpaid work done in the home such as cooking and childcare
A perspective that focuses on gender inequality in families
A conservative perspective that values the traditional nuclear family
A family formed when two people with children from previous relationships live together

cards.definition: a sociological perspective that sees society as a system where each part has a function — views the family as essential for stability
a sociological perspective based on the ideas of Karl Marx — argues society is shaped by economic inequality and class conflict
the first stage of learning society's norms and values — takes place mainly within the family
learning norms and values from institutions outside the family — e.g. school, media, peer groups
the unwritten rules about behaviour that are considered acceptable in society
the beliefs and principles shared by members of a society about what is important or desirable
the behaviours and expectations society associates with being male or female
Young and Willmott's idea that modern families have more equal sharing of domestic tasks between partners
the decline of religious influence in society — linked to declining rates of religious marriage
changes in the size and composition of a population — e.g. an ageing population, falling birth rates

questions.question: Which sociological perspective views the family as essential for maintaining social stability?
options: Marxism
Functionalism
Feminism
Postmodernism
questions.explanation: Functionalism sees society as a system where each part has a function, and the family is viewed as essential for stability and order.

questions.question: Which perspective argues that society is shaped by economic inequality and class conflict?
options: Functionalism
Interactionism
Marxism
Symbolic interactionism
questions.explanation: Marxism, based on Karl Marx's ideas, focuses on how economic systems create inequality and conflict within society.

questions.question: At what stage does primary socialisation mainly take place?
options: In school
Within the family
Through peer groups
At university
questions.explanation: Primary socialisation is the first stage of learning society's norms and values, and it takes place mainly within the family during childhood.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of secondary socialisation?
options: A baby learning to speak from their parents
A child learning to share from siblings
A teenager learning values from their school
An infant learning to eat solid food
questions.correct_answer: A teenager learning values from their school
questions.explanation: Secondary socialisation happens in institutions outside the family, such as schools, media, and peer groups.

questions.question: What are social norms?
options: Laws written in government documents
Religious teachings from churches
Unwritten rules about behaviour that are considered acceptable in society
Mathematical equations used in science
questions.correct_answer: Unwritten rules about behaviour that are considered acceptable in society
questions.explanation: Social norms are informal, unwritten expectations about how people should behave in different situations.

questions.question: What is the difference between social norms and social values?
options: They are exactly the same thing
Norms are rules for behaviour; values are beliefs about what is important or desirable
Values are rules for behaviour; norms are beliefs about what is important
Norms are only found in families; values are found everywhere
questions.correct_answer: Norms are rules for behaviour; values are beliefs about what is important or desirable
questions.explanation: Social norms are practical unwritten rules about behaviour, while social values are the deeper beliefs and principles about what matters in society.

questions.question: What term describes the behaviours and expectations society associates with being male or female?
options: Sexual orientation
Gender roles
Gender identity
Sex differences
questions.explanation: Gender roles refer to the behaviours, responsibilities, and expectations that society associates with masculinity and femininity.

questions.question: According to Young and Willmott, what is a symmetrical family?
options: A family with equal numbers of sons and daughters
A family where the mother and father have the same job
A modern family with more equal sharing of domestic tasks between partners
A family that is perfectly balanced in all ways
questions.correct_answer: A modern family with more equal sharing of domestic tasks between partners
questions.explanation: Young and Willmott's symmetrical family model suggests that in modern times, couples share household and childcare responsibilities more equally.

questions.question: What does secularisation mean?
options: An increase in religious worship
The decline of religious influence in society
The growth of religious schools
People becoming more spiritual
questions.correct_answer: The decline of religious influence in society
questions.explanation: Secularisation refers to the decrease in the importance and influence of religion in society, which is linked to declining religious marriage ceremonies.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of demographic change?
options: A change in people's fashion preferences
An increase in the number of people using social media
An ageing population with falling birth rates
A change in people's political beliefs
questions.correct_answer: An ageing population with falling birth rates
questions.explanation: Demographic change refers to changes in the size and composition of a population, such as population ageing or declining birth rates.

questions.question: Functionalism views the family as having an important role in maintaining social stability.
questions.explanation: Functionalism sees society as a system where each part, including the family, has an essential function for keeping society stable.

questions.question: Marxism focuses on the idea that economic inequality is not important in society.
questions.explanation: Marxism actually argues that economic inequality and class conflict are central to understanding how society works.

questions.question: Primary socialisation is the process of learning norms and values from institutions like schools and media.
questions.explanation: Primary socialisation occurs mainly within the family; learning from schools and media is secondary socialisation.

questions.question: Social norms are written down in law books and official documents.
questions.explanation: Social norms are unwritten, informal rules about what is considered acceptable behaviour in society.

questions.question: Gender roles are the same in every culture and society across the world.
questions.explanation: Gender roles vary significantly between different cultures and societies, showing they are socially constructed rather than universal.

questions.question: The symmetrical family model suggests that modern couples share domestic tasks and childcare more equally than in the past.
questions.explanation: Young and Willmott's symmetrical family concept describes modern families as having a more equal division of domestic labour between partners.

questions.question: Secularisation means that more people are getting married in religious ceremonies.
questions.explanation: Secularisation is the decline of religious influence, so it is actually linked to declining rates of religious marriage ceremonies.

questions.question: Demographic change includes changes in population size and composition.
questions.explanation: Demographic change refers to shifts in the size and structure of a population, such as ageing populations or changes in birth rates.

questions.question: Secondary socialisation only happens during childhood and teenage years.
questions.explanation: Secondary socialisation can occur throughout life, as people continue to learn from institutions like workplaces and community groups.

questions.question: Social values are the practical unwritten rules about how people should behave.
questions.explanation: Social values are beliefs and principles about what is important or desirable; social norms are the unwritten rules about behaviour.

questions.question: ________ is a sociological perspective that sees society as a system where each part has a function.
questions.explanation: Functionalism views society and its institutions, including the family, as working together to maintain stability and order.

questions.question: The first stage of learning society's norms and values is called ________ socialisation.
questions.explanation: Primary socialisation takes place mainly within the family and is where children first learn the norms and values of their society.

questions.question: Learning norms and values from school, media, and peer groups is an example of ________ socialisation.
questions.explanation: Secondary socialisation happens in institutions and social groups outside the family, such as schools and friendship groups.

questions.question: The unwritten rules about what is considered acceptable behaviour in society are called social ________.
questions.explanation: Social norms are the informal, unwritten expectations about how people should behave in different social situations.

questions.question: The beliefs and principles that a society values and considers important are called social ________.
questions.explanation: Social values are the shared beliefs about what is important or desirable in a society, like honesty, family, and hard work.

questions.question: The behaviours and expectations society associates with being male or female are called ________.
questions.explanation: Gender roles are the socially constructed expectations about how men and women should behave and what activities they should do.

questions.question: Young and Willmott described a ________ family as one where partners share domestic tasks more equally.
questions.explanation: The symmetrical family model describes modern families where household labour and childcare are more equally divided between partners.

questions.question: The ________ of religious influence in society has led to a decline in religious marriage ceremonies.
questions.explanation: Secularisation is the decline of religious influence in society, which is reflected in fewer people choosing religious marriages.

questions.question: Changes in the size and composition of a population, such as an ageing society, are examples of ________ change.
questions.explanation: Demographic change refers to shifts in population structure, including changes in age distribution and birth rates.

questions.question: The sociological perspective based on Karl Marx's ideas that focuses on economic inequality is called ________.
questions.explanation: Marxism analyses how economic systems create class conflict and inequality, shaping all aspects of society including the family.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A perspective that sees the family as essential for social stability
Learning society's norms and values mainly within the family
Unwritten rules about acceptable behaviour in society
Behaviours and expectations society associates with being male or female
The decline of religious influence in society

cards.definition: the idea that success is based on ability and effort, not background or privilege
a system of social stratification based on economic factors such as income, wealth, and occupation
Bourdieu's idea that middle-class children have knowledge, attitudes, and skills that give them an advantage in education
lacking the physical resources needed to succeed in education — e.g. no quiet study space, poor diet, inability to afford equipment
the idea that teachers label students based on their class, gender, or ethnicity — affecting their self-belief and achievement
when a prediction comes true because people act on the label given to them — a key concept in education sociology
grouping students by perceived ability — can reinforce social class inequalities
the informal lessons schools teach about norms, values, and behaviour — e.g. obedience to authority, punctuality
the difference in educational achievement between boys and girls — girls outperform boys in most subjects at GCSE
differences in educational attainment between ethnic groups — Chinese and Indian students tend to outperform the national average in the UK

questions.question: What does the term 'meritocracy' mean in education?
options: Success is based on ability and effort, not background or privilege
Success is guaranteed to those from wealthy families
Success depends entirely on luck and chance
Success is only possible for students in private schools
questions.correct_answer: Success is based on ability and effort, not background or privilege
questions.explanation: Meritocracy is the belief that anyone can succeed if they work hard enough, regardless of their social class or family background.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of material deprivation?
options: A student's teacher giving them a negative label
A student not having a quiet space at home to study
A student being placed in a lower ability group at school
A student not understanding the hidden curriculum
questions.correct_answer: A student not having a quiet space at home to study
questions.explanation: Material deprivation refers to lacking physical resources needed for success in education, such as study space, equipment, or nutritious food.

questions.question: What is cultural capital, according to Bourdieu?
options: Money that middle-class families have saved for education
Knowledge, attitudes, and skills that give middle-class children an advantage in education
The curriculum taught in schools about different cultures
Government funding given to schools in wealthy areas
questions.correct_answer: Knowledge, attitudes, and skills that give middle-class children an advantage in education
questions.explanation: Bourdieu argued that middle-class families pass on cultural advantages like language skills and familiarity with education that help their children succeed.

questions.question: What is labelling theory in education?
options: The process of marking students' work in class
Teachers organizing students into groups by ability
The idea that teachers label students based on their class, gender, or ethnicity, affecting their achievement
The system of giving students grades and certificates
questions.correct_answer: The idea that teachers label students based on their class, gender, or ethnicity, affecting their achievement
questions.explanation: Labelling theory suggests that when teachers form stereotypes or negative expectations about certain students, it can harm their confidence and performance.

questions.question: How does the hidden curriculum affect students?
options: It teaches official subjects like English and Maths
It teaches informal lessons about norms, values, and behaviour such as obedience and punctuality
It is a secret part of the school syllabus that only teachers know about
It provides extra qualifications that appear on students' transcripts
questions.correct_answer: It teaches informal lessons about norms, values, and behaviour such as obedience and punctuality
questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum includes the unwritten rules and values that schools teach, like respecting authority and arriving on time.

questions.question: What is a self-fulfilling prophecy in education?
options: A student predicting they will pass their exams
A teacher making a prediction about a student's future career
When a prediction comes true because people act on the label given to them
A student's parents expecting them to go to university
questions.correct_answer: When a prediction comes true because people act on the label given to them
questions.explanation: A self-fulfilling prophecy occurs when a negative label (e.g., 'You're not good at maths') causes a student to believe it and then actually perform poorly.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the gender gap in education?
options: Boys consistently outperform girls in all subjects at GCSE
Girls outperform boys in most subjects at GCSE
There is no difference in achievement between boys and girls
Only girls study subjects like English and History
questions.correct_answer: Girls outperform boys in most subjects at GCSE
questions.explanation: Current education statistics show that girls achieve higher GCSE results than boys across most subjects in the UK.

questions.question: What can streaming and setting do to students from lower social classes?
options: It helps all students succeed equally
It can reinforce social class inequalities by placing working-class students in lower groups
It has no effect on students' educational outcomes
It guarantees that all students will pass their exams
questions.correct_answer: It can reinforce social class inequalities by placing working-class students in lower groups
questions.explanation: Streaming and setting can create and maintain inequality because working-class students may be placed in lower ability groups and receive less challenging education.

questions.question: According to the flashcards, which ethnic groups tend to outperform the national average in UK education?
options: All ethnic groups perform equally
Only British students outperform the national average
Chinese and Indian students tend to outperform the national average
No ethnic group outperforms the national average
questions.correct_answer: Chinese and Indian students tend to outperform the national average
questions.explanation: Research shows that Chinese and Indian students in the UK tend to achieve higher results than the national average at GCSE.

questions.question: What is social class in the context of education?
options: A school lesson about different types of societies
A ranking system used by teachers to group students
A system of social stratification based on economic factors such as income, wealth, and occupation
The most popular students in a school year group
questions.correct_answer: A system of social stratification based on economic factors such as income, wealth, and occupation
questions.explanation: Social class divides society into groups based on money, property, and type of job, which affects educational opportunities and outcomes.

questions.question: Meritocracy means that success in education is based purely on ability and effort, regardless of background.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of meritocracy — the belief that hard work and talent, not privilege, determine success.

questions.question: Cultural capital only refers to money that families spend on education.
questions.explanation: Cultural capital includes knowledge, skills, and attitudes — not just money. It refers to the cultural advantages middle-class families pass on to their children.

questions.question: Material deprivation can include not having access to a computer or study materials at home.
questions.explanation: Material deprivation means lacking the physical resources needed for education, such as equipment, study space, or technology.

questions.question: Labelling theory suggests that teacher expectations have no impact on student achievement.
questions.explanation: Labelling theory argues that teacher labels and expectations directly affect how students see themselves and how well they perform.

questions.question: The hidden curriculum teaches students about obedience and punctuality.
questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum is the informal teaching of values and behaviour like respecting authority and arriving on time.

questions.question: A self-fulfilling prophecy happens when a positive prediction about a student always comes true by chance.
questions.explanation: A self-fulfilling prophecy occurs when people act on a label or prediction (positive or negative), making it come true through their behaviour.

questions.question: Streaming students by ability can reinforce social class inequalities in education.
questions.explanation: Streaming can reinforce inequality because working-class students may be placed in lower groups and receive less challenging education.

questions.question: Boys consistently outperform girls in most GCSE subjects in the UK.
questions.explanation: The opposite is true — girls outperform boys in most subjects at GCSE according to current educational statistics.

questions.question: Ethnicity has no impact on educational achievement in the UK.
questions.explanation: Different ethnic groups have different levels of educational achievement. For example, Chinese and Indian students tend to outperform the national average.

questions.question: Social class is determined only by how much money a person earns.
questions.explanation: Social class is based on multiple economic factors including income, wealth, and occupation, not just salary alone.

questions.question: Bourdieu's concept of ________ explains why middle-class children have advantages in education through knowledge and skills passed on by their families.
questions.explanation: Cultural capital refers to the non-financial advantages that middle-class families provide to their children.

questions.question: When a teacher's negative expectation about a student causes them to underperform, this is called a ________.
questions.explanation: A self-fulfilling prophecy is when a label or prediction causes behaviour that makes the prediction come true.

questions.question: The ________ is the informal curriculum that teaches students about obedience, punctuality, and other values.
questions.explanation: The hidden curriculum includes unwritten lessons about norms and behaviour that schools teach alongside the formal curriculum.

questions.question: ________ deprivation refers to lacking physical resources such as study space, equipment, or nutritious food needed for educational success.
questions.explanation: Material deprivation describes the lack of concrete, physical resources that students need to succeed in education.

questions.question: The belief that success is based on ability and effort rather than background or privilege is called ________.
questions.explanation: Meritocracy is the ideal that anyone can succeed through hard work and talent, regardless of their starting position.

questions.question: ________ theory suggests that when teachers label students, it affects their self-belief and educational achievement.
questions.explanation: Labelling theory examines how teacher stereotypes and expectations influence student identity and performance.

questions.question: Grouping students by perceived ability, known as ________ or setting, can reinforce social class inequalities.
questions.explanation: Streaming divides students into groups based on ability, which can maintain and deepen educational inequalities between social classes.

questions.question: In the UK, there is a ________ in education, with girls outperforming boys in most subjects at GCSE.
questions.explanation: The gender gap in education refers to the measurable difference in achievement between boys and girls, with girls currently performing better.

questions.question: ________ and ethnicity are key factors that can affect a student's educational opportunities and outcomes.
questions.explanation: Social class divides society based on income, wealth, and occupation, and significantly influences educational achievement.

questions.question: Research shows that ________ and Indian students tend to outperform the national average in UK education.
questions.explanation: Chinese and Indian ethnic groups demonstrate higher average achievement levels compared to the national average in UK schools.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: The belief that success is based on ability and effort, not background
Middle-class knowledge and skills that give children an advantage in education
Informal lessons about norms, values, and behaviour taught in schools
When a prediction comes true because people act on a label given to them
Lacking physical resources needed for educational success, such as study space or equipment

cards.definition: behaviour that breaks the law of society — defined by the state and can result in punishment
behaviour that breaks social norms but is not necessarily illegal — e.g. unusual dress, rude behaviour
the methods used by society to encourage conformity and discourage rule-breaking — formal and informal
control enforced by official institutions — e.g. police, courts, prison
control through social pressure and disapproval — e.g. family, peer groups, religion
the idea that crime occurs when people cannot achieve society's goals (e.g. wealth) through legitimate means
the idea that some groups develop alternative values that conflict with mainstream society — leading to delinquency
data on crime collected by police and the Home Office — critics argue they underrepresent the true level of crime
surveys asking people about crimes committed against them — the Crime Survey for England and Wales is the main example
crimes committed by people in professional, high-status occupations — e.g. fraud, tax evasion

questions.question: What is the definition of crime?
options: Behaviour that breaks social norms but is not necessarily illegal
Behaviour that breaks the law of society and can result in punishment
The methods used by society to encourage conformity
Unusual dress or rude behaviour that offends others
questions.correct_answer: Behaviour that breaks the law of society and can result in punishment
questions.explanation: Crime is specifically defined as behaviour that breaks the law and can lead to formal punishment. This distinguishes it from deviance, which breaks social norms but may not be illegal.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of deviance but NOT crime?
options: Robbery
Murder
Wearing unusual clothes
Burglary
questions.explanation: Wearing unusual clothes breaks social norms (deviance) but is not illegal. The other options are all crimes because they break the law.

questions.question: What is social control?
options: The punishment given by courts for breaking the law
The methods used by society to encourage conformity and discourage rule-breaking
Behaviour that breaks social norms
Crimes committed by professional people
questions.correct_answer: The methods used by society to encourage conformity and discourage rule-breaking
questions.explanation: Social control refers to all the ways society encourages people to follow rules and norms, including both formal and informal methods.

questions.question: Which is an example of formal social control?
options: Friends telling you that your behaviour is unacceptable
Your family disapproving of your choices
The police enforcing the law
Religious teachings about what is right and wrong
questions.explanation: Formal social control is enforced by official institutions like the police, courts, and prisons. The other options are examples of informal social control through social pressure.

questions.question: What is an example of informal social control?
options: Prison sentences handed down by courts
Police stopping someone for speeding
Your peer group excluding you for breaking norms
Official fines issued by the government
questions.correct_answer: Your peer group excluding you for breaking norms
questions.explanation: Informal social control works through social pressure and disapproval from groups like family and friends, rather than through official institutions.

questions.question: According to Merton's strain theory, why does crime occur?
options: Because people have alternative subcultural values
Because society does not have enough police officers
Because people cannot achieve society's goals through legitimate means
Because official crime statistics are inaccurate
questions.correct_answer: Because people cannot achieve society's goals through legitimate means
questions.explanation: Merton argued that crime happens when people want to achieve socially valued goals like wealth but cannot do so through legal methods, so they turn to crime.

questions.question: What does subcultural theory suggest?
options: Some groups develop alternative values that conflict with mainstream society
Crime is always caused by poverty
Official crime statistics are completely accurate
White-collar criminals should not be punished
questions.correct_answer: Some groups develop alternative values that conflict with mainstream society
questions.explanation: Subcultural theory explains that some groups may develop their own norms and values that differ from the wider society, which can lead to delinquency.

questions.question: What is a key criticism of official crime statistics?
options: They include too many white-collar crimes
They underrepresent the true level of crime
They are collected by victim surveys
They are based on strain theory
questions.correct_answer: They underrepresent the true level of crime
questions.explanation: Official crime statistics collected by police and the Home Office only record crimes that are reported and recorded, missing many crimes that go unreported or unrecorded.

questions.question: What are victim surveys used for?
options: To punish people who have committed crimes
To ask people about crimes committed against them
To train police officers
To determine sentences for criminals
questions.correct_answer: To ask people about crimes committed against them
questions.explanation: Victim surveys ask people directly about crimes they have experienced, providing data that may not be recorded in official statistics.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of white-collar crime?
options: Shoplifting from a supermarket
Mugging someone in the street
Tax evasion by a business owner
Vandalism of public property
questions.correct_answer: Tax evasion by a business owner
questions.explanation: White-collar crime is committed by people in professional, high-status occupations and includes offences like fraud and tax evasion.

questions.question: Deviance is always illegal.
questions.explanation: Deviance breaks social norms but is not necessarily illegal. For example, unusual dress is deviant but not a crime. Crime, however, always breaks the law.

questions.question: Formal social control is enforced by official institutions like the police and courts.
questions.explanation: Formal social control is delivered through official systems and institutions that have the power to enforce rules and punish rule-breaking.

questions.question: Family disapproval is an example of formal social control.
questions.explanation: Family disapproval is informal social control because it works through social pressure and relationships rather than through official institutions.

questions.question: According to strain theory, crime can result from the gap between goals and legitimate means.
questions.explanation: Merton's strain theory explains that when people want to achieve goals like wealth but cannot do so legally, they may turn to crime as an alternative means.

questions.question: Subcultural theory suggests that all groups in society share the same values.
questions.explanation: Subcultural theory states that some groups develop alternative values and norms that differ from mainstream society, not that everyone shares the same values.

questions.question: The Crime Survey for England and Wales is an example of a victim survey.
questions.explanation: The Crime Survey for England and Wales is the main victim survey used in the UK, asking people about crimes they have experienced.

questions.question: Official crime statistics always show the complete and accurate picture of crime.
questions.explanation: Official crime statistics are based only on reported and recorded crimes, so they underrepresent the true level of crime because many crimes go unreported.

questions.question: White-collar crime is committed by people in professional, high-status occupations.
questions.explanation: White-collar crime is specifically defined as crime committed by professionals such as business owners, accountants, and managers.

questions.question: Peer groups can be a form of informal social control.
questions.explanation: Peer groups use social pressure and disapproval to encourage conformity, making them an example of informal social control.

questions.question: All deviance is crime.
questions.explanation: Not all deviance is crime. Deviance breaks social norms, but crime specifically breaks the law. Some deviance (like unusual fashion) is not illegal.

questions.question: ________ is behaviour that breaks the law of society and can result in punishment.
questions.explanation: Crime is the key term defined as behaviour that breaks the law and can lead to formal punishment by the state.

questions.question: ________ is behaviour that breaks social norms but is not necessarily illegal.
questions.explanation: Deviance refers to behaviour that violates social norms, such as unusual dress or rudeness, without necessarily being a crime.

questions.question: The methods used by society to encourage conformity and discourage rule-breaking are called ________.
questions.explanation: Social control encompasses all the ways society encourages people to follow rules, including both formal and informal methods.

questions.question: Control enforced by official institutions such as police, courts, and prisons is called ________ social control.
questions.explanation: Formal social control is enforced by official state institutions that have legal authority to punish rule-breaking.

questions.question: Control through social pressure and disapproval from family, peer groups, and religion is called ________ social control.
questions.explanation: Informal social control works through relationships and social groups rather than official institutions, using disapproval to encourage conformity.

questions.question: Merton's ________ theory explains that crime occurs when people cannot achieve society's goals through legitimate means.
questions.explanation: Strain theory is Merton's theory that proposes crime happens when there is a gap between cultural goals and legitimate opportunities to achieve them.

questions.question: ________ theory suggests that some groups develop alternative values that conflict with mainstream society.
questions.explanation: Subcultural theory explains delinquency by showing how groups can develop norms and values different from wider society.

questions.question: Data on crime collected by police and the Home Office is called ________ crime statistics.
questions.explanation: Official crime statistics are collected by state institutions but are criticised for underrepresenting the true level of crime.

questions.question: The ________ for England and Wales is a survey that asks people about crimes committed against them.
questions.explanation: The Crime Survey for England and Wales is the main victim survey used to gather data on unreported crime.

questions.question: Crimes committed by people in professional, high-status occupations, such as fraud and tax evasion, are known as ________-collar crime.
questions.explanation: White-collar crime refers to offences committed by professionals and business people in positions of trust and authority.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Behaviour that breaks the law and can result in punishment
Control through social pressure from family, peers, and religion
Crime occurs when people cannot achieve goals through legitimate means
Crimes committed by professionals such as fraud and tax evasion
Surveys asking people about crimes committed against them

cards.definition: data in numerical form — e.g. statistics, percentages, graphs. Associated with positivism
data in words, descriptions, or meanings — e.g. interview transcripts, observations. Associated with interpretivism
a set of written questions used to collect data from a large number of people
an interview where questions are fixed in advance — produces comparable data but limits depth of response
a free-flowing interview guided by the participant — produces rich, detailed data but harder to compare
the researcher joins a group and observes them from the inside — high validity but time-consuming and raises ethical issues
the extent to which a study produces the same results if repeated — quantitative methods tend to have higher reliability
the extent to which research measures what it claims to measure — qualitative methods tend to have higher validity
how well a sample reflects the wider population — affects whether findings can be generalised
the moral principles researchers must follow — including informed consent, confidentiality, and avoiding harm to participants

questions.question: What type of data is shown in a bar chart displaying crime statistics?
options: Qualitative data
Quantitative data
Observational data
Interview data
questions.explanation: A bar chart uses numbers and statistics to represent data, which is quantitative data. Quantitative data is numerical and often associated with positivism.

questions.question: Which method would produce qualitative data?
options: A survey with 500 people
Counting the number of cars in a car park
Interview transcripts from teenagers about their social media use
Recording percentages of exam pass rates
questions.correct_answer: Interview transcripts from teenagers about their social media use
questions.explanation: Interview transcripts contain words, descriptions, and meanings rather than numbers, making them qualitative data. This type of data is associated with interpretivism.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using a questionnaire?
options: It takes a long time to complete
It can collect data from a large number of people quickly
It produces very detailed personal information
It is difficult to analyse the results
questions.correct_answer: It can collect data from a large number of people quickly
questions.explanation: Questionnaires are written sets of questions that can be distributed to many people efficiently, making them useful for collecting data from large samples.

questions.question: Which type of interview uses fixed questions prepared in advance?
options: Unstructured interview
Observation interview
Structured interview
Focus group interview
questions.explanation: A structured interview has predetermined, fixed questions that are asked to each participant in the same way. This produces comparable data but limits the depth of responses.

questions.question: What is a key disadvantage of unstructured interviews?
options: They are too quick
They produce numerical data only
The results are harder to compare between participants
They require no training
questions.correct_answer: The results are harder to compare between participants
questions.explanation: Unstructured interviews are flexible and free-flowing, which produces rich, detailed data. However, because each interview is different, comparing the results between participants is more difficult.

questions.question: What is participant observation?
options: Watching people from a distance without them knowing
The researcher joins a group and observes them from the inside
Recording observations on a questionnaire
Interviewing participants about their observations
questions.correct_answer: The researcher joins a group and observes them from the inside
questions.explanation: Participant observation involves the researcher becoming part of the group they are studying to understand their behaviour from an insider perspective. This produces high validity but is time-consuming.

questions.question: Why might participant observation raise ethical concerns?
options: It is too cheap to conduct
Participants might not know they are being studied
It uses questionnaires
It always produces quantitative data
questions.correct_answer: Participants might not know they are being studied
questions.explanation: Participant observation can raise ethical issues because the researcher may not reveal their role, meaning participants cannot give informed consent to being studied.

questions.question: Which statement best describes reliability?
options: Whether the research measures what it claims to measure
Whether a study produces the same results if repeated
Whether the sample represents the wider population
Whether participants have agreed to take part
questions.correct_answer: Whether a study produces the same results if repeated
questions.explanation: Reliability refers to consistency — if a study is repeated, reliable research should produce the same or very similar results. Quantitative methods typically have higher reliability.

questions.question: What does validity mean in research?
options: Whether the sample is large enough
Whether the questions are easy to understand
The extent to which research measures what it claims to measure
Whether the researcher follows time limits
questions.correct_answer: The extent to which research measures what it claims to measure
questions.explanation: Validity is about measuring what you intend to measure. Qualitative methods tend to have higher validity because they capture deeper meanings and authentic experiences.

questions.question: What is representativeness in research?
options: The speed at which data is collected
How well a sample reflects the wider population
Whether the researcher is trustworthy
The cost of conducting the research
questions.correct_answer: How well a sample reflects the wider population
questions.explanation: Representativeness means the sample accurately reflects the characteristics of the population being studied. Good representativeness allows findings to be generalised to the wider population.

questions.question: Quantitative data is always associated with interpretivism.
questions.explanation: Quantitative data (numerical data) is associated with positivism, not interpretivism. Qualitative data is associated with interpretivism.

questions.question: A questionnaire can collect data from a large number of people.
questions.explanation: Questionnaires are written sets of questions that can be given to hundreds or thousands of people, making them efficient for large-scale data collection.

questions.question: Structured interviews allow the researcher to follow up with unexpected answers.
questions.explanation: Structured interviews use fixed questions prepared in advance, so researchers follow a set script and cannot easily deviate to explore unexpected answers. Unstructured interviews allow this flexibility.

questions.question: Participant observation produces qualitative data.
questions.explanation: Participant observation involves detailed descriptions and observations of behaviour and social interactions, which are typically recorded as qualitative data.

questions.question: Reliability and validity mean the same thing in research.
questions.explanation: Reliability is about consistency (producing the same results when repeated), while validity is about measuring what you claim to measure. They are different concepts.

questions.question: Qualitative methods tend to have higher validity than quantitative methods.
questions.explanation: Qualitative methods capture deeper meanings and authentic experiences, giving them higher validity. Quantitative methods tend to have higher reliability instead.

questions.question: A representative sample must contain at least 10,000 participants.
questions.explanation: Representativeness is about whether the sample reflects the wider population, not about sample size. A smaller sample can be representative if selected properly.

questions.question: Informed consent is an important ethical principle in research.
questions.explanation: Informed consent means participants must be told about the study and agree to take part. This is a key ethical principle that researchers must follow.

questions.question: Confidentiality means keeping participants' identities private in research.
questions.explanation: Confidentiality protects participants by keeping their personal information and identities hidden in research reports. This is an important ethical requirement.

questions.question: Interview data is always more reliable than questionnaire data.
questions.explanation: Interviews produce qualitative data which tends to have higher validity but lower reliability. Questionnaires produce quantitative data with higher reliability. Neither is always better.

questions.question: ________ data is presented in numerical form, such as statistics and percentages.
questions.explanation: Quantitative data refers to data in numerical form that can be measured and counted, like statistics, percentages, and graphs.

questions.question: Interview transcripts and observations are examples of ________ data.
questions.explanation: Qualitative data is non-numerical data expressed in words, descriptions, and meanings, such as interview transcripts and observational notes.

questions.question: A ________ is a set of written questions used to collect data from many people.
questions.explanation: A questionnaire is a research method that uses a written set of questions to gather data efficiently from large samples of people.

questions.question: In a ________ interview, the researcher follows a fixed set of predetermined questions.
questions.explanation: A structured interview uses fixed, pre-prepared questions that are asked in the same way to each participant, producing comparable but sometimes shallow data.

questions.question: An ________ interview is flexible and free-flowing, guided by the participant's responses.
questions.explanation: An unstructured interview lacks predetermined questions and is led by the participant, producing rich, detailed data but making comparison between participants difficult.

questions.question: ________ observation involves the researcher joining a group and observing from the inside.
questions.explanation: Participant observation is where the researcher becomes a member of the group being studied to observe their behaviour and interactions from an insider perspective.

questions.question: ________ is the extent to which research produces the same results when repeated.
questions.explanation: Reliability refers to the consistency and repeatability of research findings. Quantitative methods typically have higher reliability than qualitative methods.

questions.question: ________ measures whether research actually measures what it claims to measure.
questions.explanation: Validity refers to whether research accurately measures the concept it intends to measure. Qualitative methods tend to have higher validity than quantitative methods.

questions.question: ________ refers to how well a sample reflects the characteristics of the wider population.
questions.explanation: Representativeness indicates whether a sample accurately reflects the wider population, which determines whether findings can be generalised to the whole population.

questions.question: Researchers must follow ________ principles such as informed consent and confidentiality.
questions.explanation: Ethics in research refers to the moral principles that guide researcher behaviour, including informed consent, confidentiality, avoiding harm, and treating participants fairly.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Data in numerical form such as statistics and percentages
The extent to which research measures what it claims to measure
The researcher joins a group and observes them from the inside
Participants must be told about the study and agree to take part
How well a sample reflects the wider population
